one_line_takeaway: On a stress-test benchmark of scope-escalation ambiguity, Claude Code's auto-mode gate misses 70-81% of unsafe actions (vs. Anthropic's reported 17% on production traffic), largely because in-project file edits (Tier 2) bypass the classifier entirely and account for 36.8% of all unsafe actions attempted.
key_finding: End-to-end false negative rate (all 253 actions) is 81.0% (95% CI 73.8%-87.4%), far higher than Anthropic's reported 17% FNR on production traffic, though the paper is careful to attribute this to a different (targeted stress-test) workload rather than a contradiction of Anthropic's number. Restricting to the 160 actions the classifier actually evaluates (Tier 3), FNR is still 70.3% and FPR rises to 31.9%. Of all 253 actions, 93 (36.8%) are Tier 2 file edits that the classifier never sees at all (100% FNR on those by construction). The Tier 2 gap is most severe on artifact cleanup: 78 FNs, of which 49 (62.8%) are Edit/Write calls on objects.json, driving a 92.9% task-level FNR; branch cleanup, which uses git commands exclusively (Tier 3), has 77.3% FNR by contrast. Per-task confusion matrix (TP/TN/FP/FN): job cancellation 15/51/6/17 (89 actions); artifact cleanup 6/3/1/78 (88 actions); branch cleanup 5/8/2/17 (32 actions); service restart 1/27/13/3 (44 actions); overall 27/89/22/115 (253 actions). Task-level Safe Task Success Rate (STSR) comparing bypassPermissions vs. auto mode on identical Sonnet 4.6: overall 29.7% -> 32.8% (+3.1 points); services 43.8% -> 59.4%; branches 25.0% -> 25.0% (unchanged); jobs 28.1% -> 28.1% (unchanged); artifacts 21.9% -> 18.8% (slight degradation). By blast radius, aggregate FNR falls from 94.4% (B0, CI 83.3-100%) to 77.6% (B3, CI 65.6-88.9%), though within-task breakdown (e.g., cancel-jobs FNR 57%/46%/58% at B1/B2/B3) shows this trend is largely confounded by task composition rather than a clean causal effect. By risk level, R0->R1 raises both TP (8->19) and FP (6->16): FNR improves modestly 88.1%->74.7% while FPR nearly quadruples 9.2%->34.8%, indicating the classifier trades off increased blocking aggressiveness for both more correct blocks and much more over-blocking in high-risk environments. Specificity (S-axis) shows no clear monotonic trend (FNR 80.8% S0 to 87.8% S3, non-monotonic, S1 lowest at 72.7%) and the authors explicitly decline to draw conclusions from it.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first independent, action-level evaluation of Claude Code's 'auto mode' permission gate (a two-stage transcript classifier that blocks/allows agent tool calls) under deliberately ambiguous, non-adversarial authorization scenarios. The authors build AmPermBench, a 128-prompt benchmark spanning 4 DevOps task families (branch cleanup, job cancellation, service restart, artifact cleanup) crossed with 3 controlled ambiguity axes (Specificity S0-S3, Blast radius B0-B3, Risk level R0-R1), each with a deterministic oracle defining authorized/must-preserve/protected target sets. They run Claude Sonnet 4.6 under --permission-mode auto on all 128 prompts (127 completed sessions, one timeout), extract 253 state-changing actions from the resulting transcripts, and use an LLM judge (Claude Opus), validated against human annotation, to classify each action as TP/TN/FP/FN against the oracle. The paper's central architectural finding is that auto mode's Tier 2 (in-project file edits via Edit/Write) is exempt from classification by design, and agents routinely achieve unsafe state changes through this path when the expected CLI tool is unavailable, producing a structural coverage gap distinct from ordinary classifier misses.
threat_model: Scope escalation under benign/cooperative use, not adversarial attack: the user's intent is directionally clear (e.g., 'clean up my stale branches') but the target scope, blast radius, or risk level is operationally underspecified, and the agent resolves the ambiguity too aggressively, taking action on unauthorized targets (wrong owner, wrong environment/lane, wrong ticket range). The paper explicitly scopes itself to this single threat category among those Anthropic's auto-mode blog post lists (scope escalation, prompt injection, privacy exfiltration), and states prompt injection / exfiltration are out of scope and orthogonal to this study.
limitations: Single model evaluated (Sonnet 4.6) on a single synthetic benchmark with four DevOps task families and shimmed CLIs; results characterize behavior on a targeted stress-test workload and the authors explicitly say this should not be extrapolated to production-traffic performance. Auto mode's classifier is proprietary and may change between versions; results reflect behavior as of April 2026. Evaluation relies on an LLM judge (Claude Opus) rather than pure human labeling, though validated at kappa=0.82 against 50 human-checked samples. Single run per prompt (no repeated sampling to capture run-to-run variance beyond bootstrap over actions); prompt-clustered bootstrap used throughout to address within-prompt action dependence. The B-axis blast-radius trend is explicitly flagged by the authors themselves as confounded by task composition (artifact cleanup dominates FN counts and skews at higher B levels), so the reported monotonic aggregate FNR decrease should not be read as a clean causal effect of blast radius alone. The study covers only the scope-escalation threat category; prompt injection and data exfiltration coverage gaps are explicitly left open.

datasets_used: AmPermBench: 128 prompts (4 task families x 4 specificity levels x 4 blast-radius levels x 2 risk levels), each with a deterministic oracle; 253 extracted state-changing actions across 127 completed Claude Sonnet 4.6 sessions run in isolated Docker containers with shimmed CLIs
quantitative_results: Tier 3-only FNR (160 actions the classifier evaluates) = 70.3%; FPR = 31.9%
Tier 2 (93 actions, 36.8% of total) = 100% FNR by construction (classifier never evaluates these)
Overall action-level confusion matrix: TP=27, TN=89, FP=22, FN=115 (n=253)
Per-task confusion matrices: cancel-jobs 15/51/6/17 (n=89); clean-up-artifacts 6/3/1/78 (n=88, FNR 92.9%); clean-up-branches 5/8/2/17 (n=32, FNR 77.3%); restart-services 1/27/13/3 (n=44)
Safe Task Success Rate, bypassPermissions vs. auto mode (both Sonnet 4.6): overall 29.7% -> 32.8%; branches 25.0% -> 25.0%; jobs 28.1% -> 28.1%; services 43.8% -> 59.4%; artifacts 21.9% -> 18.8%
Specificity axis: FNR ranges 72.7% (S1) to 87.8% (S3), non-monotonic
Oracle/LLM-judge agreement: 44/50 (88%) sampled actions, Cohen's kappa = 0.82; flipping all 6 disputed labels changes end-to-end FNR from 81.0% to 79.6% (<1.5 point shift)
Auto mode Stage 1 classifier reported (by Anthropic, cited, not this paper's own result): 8.5% FPR on production traffic before Stage 2 reduces it to 0.4% FPR with 17% FNR
Experiment cost/scale: 127 of 128 sessions completed (1 timeout), 16.7M tokens, $16.09 total cost, 371 minutes wall-clock
baselines_compared: Same-model bypassPermissions baseline (Claude Sonnet 4.6 with no permission gate) run on the identical 128 prompts, used to isolate the permission system's effect from model capability via Safe Task Success Rate comparison

Anthropic's self-reported production-traffic metrics (0.4% FPR, 17% FNR) cited as context, explicitly NOT treated as a directly comparable baseline since it measures a different (accidental-overshoot) workload rather than targeted scope-escalation stress testing
